1: vezeh hadavar asher ta'aseh lahem leqadesh otam: 'this is what you shall do for them to consecrate them.' The verb qadash (H6942, to be holy, set apart, consecrate) is the root of qodesh (holiness) and miqdash (sanctuary). Consecration transfers a person or object from the common sphere to the sphere of divine holiness — a one-way, irreversible transfer. The consecration ceremony of Exod 29 is the most elaborate personal consecration ritual in the OT. The ingredients: one young bull (par ben-baqar echad) and two unblemished rams (aylim tamimim) — sin offering, first burnt offering, and ram of ordination.
2: lachmim matzot vegalot matzot belulot bashemen: 'unleavened bread, unleavened loaves mixed with oil, and unleavened wafers spread with oil' — made from fine wheat flour (solet), the highest grade of flour. The three forms of unleavened bread (loaves, thick cakes, wafers) represent different textures of the same basic offering material: the absence of leaven in all three forms signals the purity of the priestly consecration. All three forms appear in the NT's eucharistic symbolism: Christ as the living bread (John 6:35), the bread of life made without the leaven of sin (1 Corinthians 5:7-8).
3: vesamta otam al-sal echad vehigashta otam basal: 'place them in a basket and present them along with the bull and the two rams.' The basket (sal, H5536) holds the three bread types; the animals stand alongside. The combined presentation — animal blood sacrifices + grain offerings — represents the complete Levitical system: blood (atonement and consecration) + grain (thank-offering and dependence). The basket of bread reappears in Lev 8 (the actual ordination ceremony following these instructions) and in the ordination of the Nazirite (Numbers 6:15-17).
4: veRachatzta otam bamayim: 'wash them with water' — the first act of the consecration is ritual washing at the entrance of the tent of meeting. Rachatz (H7364, to wash, bathe) — full body immersion. The washing before investiture is the liturgical logic of baptism: you must be washed before you are clothed with the priestly garments. John's baptism in the Jordan and Christian baptism both participate in this ritual logic — washing precedes anointing, anointing precedes service. Hebrews 10:22 applies the vestment-washing to the believer's entrance before God: 'having our hearts sprinkled clean from an evil conscience and our bodies washed with pure water.'
5: velaqachta et-habegadim vehilbashta et-Aharon et-hakutonet ve'et-me'il ha'ephod: 'take the garments and dress Aaron with the tunic, the robe of the ephod, the ephod, and the breastpiece, binding it with the artistic band of the ephod.' The investiture proceeds from the inside out: linen tunic → blue robe → ephod → breastpiece → artistic band. Each layer is donned in order; the sequence is not arbitrary. The layered investiture of the priest pictures the layered righteousness of the believer in Christ: each aspect of Christ's person (righteousness, intercession, mediation) is “put on” in the union with him. Galatians 3:27 uses the investiture language: 'as many of you as were baptized into Christ have put on Christ.'
6: vesamta hamitznefet al-rosho venatata et-nezer haqodesh al-hamitznefet: 'put the turban on his head and attach the holy crown/sacred emblem to the turban.' The nezer haqodesh (H5145, sacred crown/consecration) is the gold frontlet of Exodus 28:36-38 — KODESH L'YHWH. The term nezer is also used for the Nazirite's consecration and for the king's crown. The priest's turban-crown blends royal and sacred symbolism: the high priest wears a crown of dedication, not of political power. The same convergence of crown and consecration appears in Zechariah's vision of the high priest crowned as king-priest (Zechariah 6:11-13) — directly prophesying the Melchizedekian Christ who combines both offices.
7: veliaqachta et-shemen hamishchah veyatzaqta al-rosho umashachta oto: 'take the anointing oil and anoint him by pouring it on his head.' The pouring of the anointing oil on the head is the act that makes the priest a mashiach (anointed one). The word Messiah (Hebrew) and Christ (Greek Christos) both mean “the Anointed One.” Jesus's baptism by the Spirit (Matthew 3:16; Acts 10:38: 'God anointed Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Spirit and with power') is the NT fulfillment of the high-priestly anointing: Jesus is consecrated to his high-priestly ministry by the Spirit descending on him like the oil poured on Aaron's head (Psalms 133:2 applies the head-anointing to the unity of the covenant community). The Spirit as the divine anointing-oil is a consistent NT metaphor (1 John 2:20, 27: chrisma, anointing from the Holy One).
8: veet-banav taqriv vehilbashta otam kutnot: 'bring his sons and dress them in tunics.' The sons of Aaron receive investiture immediately after their father — the priestly order is transmitted from generation to generation. The dynastic structure of the Aaronic priesthood (each generation ordained by the previous one) provides temporary continuity but is structurally limited: mortal priests cannot serve permanently. Hebrews 7:23-25 contrasts: 'the former priests were many in number, because they were prevented by death from continuing in office, but he holds his priesthood permanently, because he continues forever.'
9: veyatzarta lahem mitznefot vesamta lahem avnetim: 'put the caps on them and tie sashes on them.' Vehayetah lahem kehunah lechuqat olam: 'the priesthood shall be theirs by a permanent statute' — chuqat olam (eternal statute). The Aaronic priesthood is declared a permanent, eternal covenant. But the NT reveals that “eternal” here means the principle endures forever, not the specific Aaronic line: Hebrews 7:12 explains that when the priesthood changes, the law necessarily changes too. The chuqat olam of the Aaronic priesthood is fulfilled and superseded in the eternal priesthood of Christ according to the order of Melchizedek (Psalms 110:4; Hebrews 7:24).
10: veHigashta et-haPar lifnei Ohel Mo'ed vesamach Aharon uvanav et-yedeihem al-rosh haPar: 'bring the bull before the tent of meeting and Aaron and his sons shall lay their hands on the bull's head.' The laying on of hands (samach yadav, H5564) is the act of identification and transference — the one placing hands on the animal's head transfers their identity, sin, or blessing to the animal. The sin offering's hand-laying makes the bull the legal representative of the offerers: it dies in their place. This is the OT's clearest enacted statement of substitutionary atonement. Hebrews 9:7 connects this: the high priest goes into the holy of holies with blood 'which he offers for himself and for the sins of the people committed in ignorance.'
11: veshachata et-haPar lifnei YHWH petach Ohel Mo'ed: 'slaughter the bull before YHWH at the entrance to the tent of meeting.' The slaughter at the entrance (petach) to the tent of meeting — not inside, not at a distance — places the death at the threshold between the common and the holy. The animal dies at the boundary where the holy and the common meet. Christ dies at the threshold of the age — the “entrance” between the old covenant world and the new creation — as the definitive sin offering for all humanity (Hebrews 9:26: 'he has appeared once for all at the end of the ages to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself').
12: velaqachta middam haPar venatata al-qarnot hamizbeach be'etzba'echa: 'take some of the bull's blood and put it on the horns of the altar with your finger.' The horns of the altar (qarnot hamizbeach) are the four projecting corners of the altar — the places where a fugitive would grab for sanctuary (1 Kings 1:50: Adonijah grabbed the altar horns). Putting blood on the altar's horns applies the atonement to the very seat of divine power and mercy. The remainder of the blood is poured out (shafach) at the base of the altar — consecrating the foundation. The base-pouring of blood anticipates John 19:34: the blood and water poured out at the foot of the cross, at the very base of the ultimate altar.
13: velaqachta et-kol-hahelev hamekhaseh et-haqerev: 'take all the fat surrounding the inner organs, the lobe of the liver, and both kidneys with their fat, and burn them on the altar.' The burning of the fat and inner organs is the portion reserved for YHWH (Leviticus 3:16-17: 'all fat is YHWH's'). The fat (chelev) represents the richest, most vital part of the animal — the offering to YHWH is not the leftover but the best portion. The liver and kidneys in the ANE were understood to be the seat of the will and emotions (the same function assigned to the heart in the NT). The inner organs represent the deepest interior of the animal — its hidden inner life offered to God.
14: ve'et-besar haPar ve'et-oro ve'et-pirsho tisrof ba'esh michutz lamachaneh chatat hu: 'burn the bull's flesh, hide, and intestines outside the camp; it is a sin offering.' The sin offering's carcass must be burned outside the camp (michutz lamachaneh) — not on the altar, not inside the tabernacle precincts, but expelled to the unclean place. Hebrews 13:11-13 draws the explicit application: 'the bodies of those animals whose blood is brought into the holy places by the high priest as a sacrifice for sin are burned outside the camp. So Jesus also suffered outside the gate in order to sanctify the people through his own blood. Therefore let us go to him outside the camp.' The outside-the-camp burning of the sin offering points directly to Golgotha.
15: ve'et-ha'ayil ha'echad tiqach vesamach Aharon uvanav et-yedeihem al-rosh ha'ayil: 'take one of the rams and Aaron and his sons shall lay their hands on its head.' The first ram is the olah (burnt offering) — the whole-body offering entirely consumed by fire, nothing kept back. The laying of hands on the burnt offering is the same act of identification as with the sin offering: the offerer is fully identified with the sacrifice, not merely presenting a gift from outside. The olah is total consecration — the entire animal ascending to YHWH. Romans 12:1 applies the same language to the NT believer: 'present your bodies as a living sacrifice (thusian zosan), holy and acceptable to God.'
16: veshachata et-ha'ayil velaqachta et-damo vezaraqta al-hamizbeach saviv: 'slaughter the ram and take its blood and throw it against all sides of the altar.' The blood-throwing (zaraq, H2236 — to toss, scatter) covers all four sides of the altar — a comprehensive, 360-degree consecration. The blood of the olah consecrates the altar itself, not just the offerers. The altar must be purified and dedicated before it can serve as the perpetual meeting-place between Israel and YHWH. The priestly ordination consecrates not only the priests but the physical structure through which they minister.
17: ve'et-ha'ayil tenateach linetiachav vekibalta et-qirbo: 'cut the ram into pieces and wash the inner organs and legs, and put them with the rest of the pieces.' The dismembering of the sacrifice and the washing of the organs and legs ensures that the offering is complete, clean, and properly prepared. The washing removes the blood and waste products that belong to the earthly life of the animal; what is then burned is the clean body of the animal. The attention to cleanliness in the offering material is consistent: YHWH receives what is washed, clean, and wholly given.
18: vehiqtarta et-kol-ha'ayil hamizbeachah olah hu l'YHWH reyach nichoach isheh l'YHWH hu: 'burn the entire ram on the altar as a burnt offering to YHWH; it is a pleasing aroma, a food offering to YHWH.' Reyach nichoach (H5207, a soothing/pleasing aroma) — the standard formula for acceptable sacrifice in the Levitical system (Leviticus 1:9, 13, 17; Exodus 2:2, 9, etc.). Paul applies the same phrase to Christ's self-offering: 'Christ loved us and gave himself up for us, a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God' (Ephesians 5:2 — Greek: osmēn euōdias, exact translation of reyach nichoach). The burnt offering's pleasing aroma is the OT's most vivid statement that sacrifice is not merely transaction but relationship — a gift that pleases God.
19: velaqachta et-ha'ayil hasheni vesamach Aharon uvanav et-yedeihem al-rosh ha'ayil: 'take the second ram and Aaron and his sons shall lay their hands on its head.' The second ram is the eil milluim (ram of ordination/filling) — a sacrifice specific to the ordination ceremony, with no equivalent in the regular sacrificial calendar. Milluim (H4394, ordination, from male, to fill) — the filling of the hands (Exodus 29:24) with the wave offering. The ram of ordination is the sacrifice whose portions are literally placed in the priests' hands as the defining act of priestly commissioning. The priests “receive” the sacrifice before they can offer it.
20: veNatata al-tenuch ozen Aharon hayemanit ve'al-bohen yad-yemino ve'al-bohen raglo heymanit: 'put some of its blood on the lobe of the right ear of Aaron, and on the thumb of his right hand, and on the big toe of his right foot.' The three blood applications — ear, hand, foot — consecrate the three primary means of priestly service: (1) right ear: consecrated hearing of YHWH's word — the priest must hear YHWH's instructions accurately before he can serve; (2) right thumb/hand: consecrated doing of priestly work — the hands handle the holy things; (3) right big toe/foot: consecrated walking — the priest's movement in holy space must be holy. The same three-point application appears in the cleansed leper's ritual (Leviticus 14:14-17) — total bodily consecration from hearing to acting to going. Christ consecrates believers entirely: 'he who believes in me ... streams of living water' — from hearing (ear) to acting (hand) to going (foot).
21: velaqachta middam asher al-hamizbeach umishemen hamishchah vezaraqta al-Aharon veal-begadav: 'take some of the blood on the altar and some of the anointing oil and sprinkle it on Aaron and his garments.' The priestly vestments are themselves consecrated with the mixture of altar blood and anointing oil — the same combination that consecrates the priests. Altar-blood represents atonement; oil represents the anointing Spirit. The vestments, like the priests, are consecrated by the convergence of sacrifice and Spirit. Paul's 'Christ who is in you' (Colossians 1:27) and 'clothed with Christ' (Galatians 3:27) apply both the vestment-language and the consecration-language to the believer: in Christ they are covered by his blood and anointed by his Spirit.
22: velaqachta min-ha'ayil hachelev vehaalyah: 'take from the ram the fat, the fat tail, the fat around the internal organs, the long lobe of the liver, both kidneys with their fat, and the right thigh — for it is the ram of ordination.' The ram of ordination yields a unique set of portions: the fat, the liver lobe, the kidneys (YHWH's portion in regular offerings) plus the right thigh (shok hayamin) — which is the priest's regular share — plus all three bread types from the basket. These portions are first placed in the priests' hands (milluim) and then burned on the altar, creating a unique layered offering where priest and altar share the same sacrifice before YHWH.
23: vekhikar lechem achat vechalat shemen achat verakiq echad misal hamatzot asher lifnei YHWH: 'from the basket of unleavened bread before YHWH take one loaf, one cake mixed with oil, and one wafer.' The three bread types (loaf, thick cake, wafer) are represented by one of each — a sample of the whole offering given to the ordination ceremony. The combination of animal sacrifice + bread in the wave offering anticipates the Last Supper's combination of the lamb (body) and bread: the priestly ordination meal is a type of the ultimate covenant meal where Christ is simultaneously priest, offering, and host.
24: vesamta et-hakol al-kapei Aharon ve'al-kapei vanav: 'put all these in the hands of Aaron and his sons' — this is the milu yad (filling of the hands) that gives ordination its name in Hebrew (milluim). The priests' hands are literally filled with the ordination sacrifice: they hold the offering before YHWH, and YHWH accepts it through Moses's burning it. The verb henif (to wave, H5130) describes the tenufah (wave offering): the priest holds the offering and waves it before YHWH, presenting it as an act of giving before it is taken for burning. The priest gives before God, then receives back his portion — the pattern of priestly ministry: total self-offering that results in received portion.
25: velaqachta otam miyedam veehiqtarta hamizbeachah al-ha'olah lireyach nichoach lifnei YHWH: 'take them from their hands and burn them on the altar on top of the burnt offering, as a pleasing aroma before YHWH.' The ordination wave offering is placed on top of the regular burnt offering (olah) — the ordination of the priests is built on the foundation of the regular sacrifice. The priest cannot be ordained apart from sacrifice; his entire ministry emerges from the sacrificial foundation. The Christological application: Jesus's priestly ordination is inseparable from his sacrifice — he 'offered himself' (Hebrews 9:14), the priest and the offering being the same person.
26: velaqachta et-hecazeh min-eil hamillui'im asher le'Aharon vehenfta oto tenufah lifnei YHWH vehayah lecha lamanah: 'take the breast of Aaron's ordination ram and wave it before YHWH as a wave offering; it shall be your portion.' Moses receives the breast of the ordination ram — the one conducting the ordination receives a portion of the ordination sacrifice. The distribution of the sacrifice to God (the burning portions), the priests (the thigh), and the officiant (the breast) pictures the communal nature of the ordination: no one in the covenant community is outside the benefits of the priestly sacrifice. The sharing of the ordination sacrifice models the NT's eucharistic logic: the communion meal is participated in by all, distributing Christ's benefits to the whole community.
27: veqidashta et-chasah hatenufah ve'et-shok hatrumah: 'consecrate those parts of the ordination ram: the breast of the wave offering and the thigh of the contribution.' The wave offering (tenufah) and the heave/contribution offering (terumah) are the two types of elevated offerings — elevated by waving and by lifting. Terumah (H8641, from rum, to be high, exalted) — the offering that is lifted up. The lifting of the offering to YHWH mirrors Christ's exaltation: 'when I am lifted up from the earth, I will draw all people to myself' (John 12:32) — the cross as the ultimate terumah, the offering lifted up toward YHWH for the people's benefit.
28: vehayah le'Aharon ulvanav lechok-olam meyit benei Yisrael ki terumah hu: 'it shall belong to Aaron and his sons as a perpetual ordinance from the Israelites, for it is a contribution.' The priests' portions from the offerings are their regular income — they have no land inheritance in Israel (Numbers 18:20-24: 'I am your portion and your inheritance'). The priests live from the altar. Paul applies this in 1 Corinthians 9:13-14: 'those who serve at the altar share in the altar's offerings; in the same way, the Lord commanded that those who proclaim the gospel should get their living by the gospel.' The priestly dependence on the congregation's offerings is the OT background for the NT minister's financial support.
29: ubigdei hakodesh asher le'Aharon yihyu livanav acharav: 'Aaron's sacred garments will belong to his descendants after him, so they may be anointed in them and ordained in them.' The high priestly vestments are dynastic — passed from father to son at each generational transition. The vestments outlast the individual priest; the office is greater than the officeholder. This dynastic transmission emphasizes the continuity of the mediatorial role across generations — the same garments that Aaron wore, his sons will wear, and their sons after them. Hebrews contrasts: Christ wears his “vestment” permanently because he lives permanently; his garment of holiness is intrinsic, not transmitted from outside.
30: shivat yamim yilbasham hakohen tachtav mibanaav: 'for seven days the son who succeeds him as priest shall wear them when he comes to the tent of meeting to minister in the holy place.' The seven-day investiture of the successor high priest ensures continuity and proper consecration before service begins. Seven days mirrors the creation week — the ordination period creates a new “world” of priestly reality. The completeness of seven (sheva, from shava, to be complete) frames the ordination as a complete act. The seven-day ordination of Aaron and his sons (Leviticus 8:33-36) becomes the pattern for the seven-day second-temple ordination described by Josephus.
31: ve'et eil hamillui'im tiqach uvishalta et-besaro bemakom qadosh: 'take the ordination ram and cook the meat in a sacred place.' The ordination meal is eaten in the sacred space — the priests eat the sacrificial meat at or near the tent of meeting, not at home. The sacred meal must be consumed in the sacred space: the meal's holiness is inseparable from its location. The new covenant's sacred meal (the Eucharist) is similarly a gathered meal in the community's sacred assembly (1 Corinthians 11:17-34: 'when you come together as a church'), not a private act.
32: ve'achal Aharon uvanav et-besar ha'ayil ve'et-halachem asher basal al-petach Ohel Mo'ed: 'Aaron and his sons shall eat the meat of the ram and the bread in the basket at the entrance of the tent of meeting.' The ordination meal is eaten at the threshold — between the outer court and the tent's interior. The placement at the petach (entrance) is significant: the priests eat where YHWH meets Israel. Their meal is a covenant meal at the meeting-point. The priest's food is inseparably connected to his ministry location: his sustenance comes from the place of mediation, not from ordinary life outside the sacred precincts.
33: ve'achlu otam asher kupar bahem lemale et-yadam lekadshem: 'they shall eat the things by which atonement was made for their ordination and consecration.' The ordination meal is the atonement meal — the priests eat the very sacrifice that consecrated them. The eating of the sacrifice is participation in its effect: those who eat the ordination meal participate in the atonement it accomplished. This is the theological logic of the Eucharist: 'the cup of blessing ... is it not participation (koinōnia) in the blood of Christ? The bread that we break ... is it not participation in the body of Christ?' (1 Corinthians 10:16). Eating the sacrifice is covenant participation.
34: ve'im yivater mibasar hamilluim u-min-halachem ad-haboqer vesarfeta et-hanotar ba'esh: 'if any of the ordination meat or bread remains until morning, burn it; it shall not be eaten, for it is holy.' The prohibition on keeping ordination-meal leftovers until morning mirrors the Passover prohibition (Exodus 12:10) — both are sacred meals consumed in their entirety. The fire-disposal of the remainder ensures no desecration: what was consecrated to YHWH cannot become ordinary food. The holiness of the ordination meal demands total consumption — it cannot linger into the ordinary world of the next day.
35: veasita le'Aharon ulvanav kachah ka'asher tziviti otach shivat yamim temaleh yadam: 'do for Aaron and his sons everything I have commanded you; take seven days to ordain them.' The seven-day ordination period (repeated in Leviticus 8:33-36) creates a liminal space: the priests are neither ordinary Israelites nor fully functioning ministers until the seven days are complete. Liminal periods of transition are structurally important throughout Scripture: seven days of creation, seven days of the feast of unleavened bread, seven days for leprosy diagnosis and purification, and the seven weeks between Passover and Pentecost. The ordination's seven days frame the priestly creation as a new cosmological reality.
36: upar chatat ta'aseh layom al-hakippurim: 'a bull as a sin offering you shall sacrifice each day for atonement.' During the seven ordination days, a daily sin offering (bull) is sacrificed to make atonement for the altar itself. Kippur (atonement, H3723) — from kapar (to cover, propitiate, atone). The altar must be atoned for because it has been used by sinful people and in a sinful world; the repeated sin offerings during the ordination week purify the altar to the point of becoming most holy (qodesh qodashim, Exodus 29:37). The need for seven days of atonement for the altar pictures the comprehensive purification that the OT sacrificial system was designed to achieve but could not permanently accomplish — only Christ's once-for-all sacrifice achieves permanent atonement (Hebrews 10:14).
37: shivat yamim tekaper al-hamizbeach veqidashta oto vehayah hamizbeach qodesh qodashim: 'for seven days you shall make atonement for the altar and consecrate it; then the altar will be most holy.' Qodesh qodashim (most holy, H6944 + H6944) — the highest degree of holiness in the Levitical system. The altar becomes so holy that whatever touches it becomes holy (Exodus 29:37b: 'whatever touches the altar shall become holy'). This holiness-transfer from altar to object is the converse of contamination-transfer from impure to pure: holiness spreads by contact upward. The NT's application: 'do you not know that your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit?' (1 Corinthians 6:19) — the believer who is united to Christ becomes holy by contact with his holiness.
38: vezeh asher ta'aseh al-hamizbeach kevasim benei-shanah shnayim layom tamid: 'this is what you are to offer on the altar regularly each day: two year-old lambs.' The tamid offering (H8548, continual/perpetual) is the two daily lambs — one in the morning, one at twilight (Exodus 29:39) — that constitute the foundational rhythm of Israel's worship. Tamid means “always, continually, perpetually” — the offering that never stops. The daily sacrifice frames all other sacrifices (Sabbath, monthly, festival) within its perpetual rhythm. Hebrews 7:27 contrasts Christ with the daily Levitical offering: 'he has no need, like those high priests, to offer sacrifices daily ... he did this once for all when he offered up himself.' The daily tamid's perpetual repetition is replaced by Christ's single unrepeatable offering.
39: et-hakeves ha'echad ta'aseh vaboqer ve'et hakeves hasheni ta'aseh ben ha'arbayim: 'offer one lamb in the morning and the other at twilight.' The morning and evening framing of the daily sacrifice marks the entire day as belonging to YHWH — from first light (boqer) to twilight (ben ha'arbayim, between the two evenings), the rhythm of the day is bounded by sacrifice. The morning-to-evening sacrificial frame is the OT's way of consecrating time itself. Jesus's crucifixion at the hour of the evening tamid (the ninth hour, 3 PM — the hour of the evening sacrifice, Acts 3:1: 'the hour of prayer, the ninth hour') is the final tamid that makes all previous ones obsolete.
40: ve'esaron solet belul berishit kativ shekel shemen berit: 'with the first lamb offer a tenth of an ephah of fine flour mixed with a quarter of a hin of pressed oil.' The grain offering (minchah) accompanies the daily lamb — the blood offering is not alone but paired with the grain and drink offerings (Exodus 29:40-41). The three-element structure of the daily offering (lamb + flour/oil + wine) mirrors the three elements of the covenant meal: meat, bread, and wine. The Last Supper echoes all three: the Passover lamb (body), the bread (grain), and the cup (wine) — the daily sacrifice's three elements are concentrated in the new covenant meal.
41: ve'et-hakeves hasheni ta'aseh ben ha'arbayim keminchah haboqer ucheniskah ta'aseh lo lireyach nichoach: 'offer the second lamb at twilight with the same grain and drink offerings as in the morning, as a pleasing aroma, a food offering to YHWH.' The morning and evening offerings are identical in composition — the day is framed symmetrically by YHWH's service. The reyach nichoach formula (pleasing aroma) applied to the evening offering as well as the morning shows that both ends of the day are equally acceptable to YHWH. The day's entire span from dawn to dusk is offered to YHWH through the tamid rhythm.
42: olat tamid ledoroteichem petach Ohel Mo'ed lifnei YHWH asher iva'ed lachem shamah ledaber eleycha sham: 'this is the regular burnt offering throughout your generations at the entrance to the tent of meeting before YHWH, where I will meet with you to speak with you there.' The olat tamid (perpetual burnt offering) is the appointed meeting place between YHWH and Moses/Israel — YHWH meets Israel at the site and time of the daily sacrifice. The tent of meeting (ohel mo'ed) gets its name from this meeting function: it is the place where YHWH and Israel keep their appointment, and the appointment is kept through the continual offering. The NT fulfillment: Christ is 'our meeting place' (Ephesians 2:18: 'through him we have access to the Father') — the perpetual access to YHWH that the daily sacrifice regulated is now permanently opened through Christ.
43: venikdash bikhvodi: 'I will be sanctified by my glory' — YHWH will make the tent holy by his own glory (kavod) dwelling there. The divine glory is both the agent and the content of the sanctuary's holiness: YHWH does not need human action to make himself holy, but he condescends to make the tent holy by his presence. The kavod (glory) of YHWH fills the tent at its dedication (Exodus 40:34-35: 'the cloud covered the tent of meeting and the glory of YHWH filled the tabernacle'). John 1:14's 'the Word became flesh and dwelt (eskēnōsen, “tabernacled”) among us, and we have seen his glory (doxan)' is the direct fulfillment of this promise: the divine kavod that filled the tabernacle now dwells in human flesh.
44: veqidashti et-Ohel Mo'ed ve'et-hamizbeach ve'et-Aharon ve'et-banav aqadesh lekahane li: 'I will consecrate the tent of meeting and the altar, and Aaron and his sons I will consecrate to minister to me as priests.' The direct divine consecration of the tent, altar, and priests — all three receive YHWH's own sanctification, not merely human dedication. The priests are consecrated by YHWH himself, not only by Moses's ceremony. The sequence of chapters 28-29 moves from human instruction (what to make and do) to divine action (YHWH consecrates directly) — the human obedience is the necessary condition, but YHWH's act is the sufficient cause of holiness.
45: veshachanti betoch benei Yisrael vehaytyi lahem le'Elohim: 'I will dwell (shachan) among the Israelites and be their God.' The central promise of the entire tabernacle construction project (Exodus 25-31, 35-40) is expressed in two brief clauses: YHWH will dwell among his people, and YHWH will be their God. Shachan (H7931, to dwell, tabernacle, rest) is the root of mishkan (tabernacle, dwelling) and of the later Rabbinic concept of the Shekinah (divine presence). John 1:14's 'he tabernacled (eskēnōsen) among us' — the verb is a direct echo of shachan — announces the fulfillment: Jesus is the true tabernacle, the embodiment of YHWH's dwelling among his people.
46: veydeu ki ani YHWH eloheihem asher hotzeti otam meeretz Mitzrayim leshochni betocham ani YHWH eloheihem: 'they will know that I am YHWH their God, who brought them out of Egypt so that I might dwell (shochni) among them; I am YHWH their God.' The final verse of the chapter gives the purpose of the entire Exodus: YHWH brought Israel out of Egypt not merely to give them freedom or land, but specifically leshochni betocham ('so that I might dwell among them'). The Exodus's goal is YHWH's indwelling presence. The tabernacle and its elaborate priestly system is the mechanism of this indwelling. Christ's incarnation is the fulfillment of the Exodus's stated purpose: God comes out of Egypt (Matthew 2:15: 'out of Egypt I called my Son') to dwell permanently among his people — not in a tent but in human flesh, and ultimately in the Spirit poured out at Pentecost and dwelling in every believer (1 Corinthians 3:16; Exodus 6:19).
